questions.prompt: The admittance of a parallel RLC circuit is:
questions.explanation: Parallel elements: admittances add. Y = G + jBC + jBL = 1/R + jωC + 1/jωL = 1/R + j(ωC − 1/ωL).

questions.prompt: At parallel resonance, the impedance is:
questions.explanation: At resonance, Y = 1/R (minimum), so Z = 1/Y = R (maximum). Opposite of series, where Z = R is minimum.

questions.prompt: The parallel LC combination at resonance acts as:
questions.explanation: Susceptances cancel (BC + BL = 0), total admittance of LC = 0, impedance = ∞ → open circuit. Called a 'tank circuit.'

questions.prompt: The resonant frequency of a parallel RLC circuit is:
questions.explanation: ω₀ = 1/√LC for both series and parallel. The resonant frequency depends only on L and C.

questions.prompt: The bandwidth of a parallel RLC circuit is:
questions.explanation: Parallel bandwidth B = 1/RC (dual of series B = R/L). Also B = ω₀/Q.

questions.prompt: In the duality mapping, series L maps to parallel:
questions.explanation: Duality swaps L ↔ C and R ↔ 1/R. Series inductor maps to parallel capacitor.

questions.prompt: Current magnification in parallel resonance means:
options: IL and IC can be Q times the source current
questions.correctAnswer: IL and IC can be Q times the source current
questions.explanation: Dual of voltage magnification in series. At parallel resonance, IL = IC = Q × Is, circulating internally in the tank.

questions.prompt: The quality factor of a parallel RLC circuit is:
questions.explanation: By duality: series Q = ωoL/R → parallel Q = ωoC·(1/(1/R)) = ωoRC. Also Q = R/ωoL.

questions.prompt: Why is a parallel LC circuit called a 'tank circuit'?
options: Energy oscillates (tanks) between L and C at resonance
questions.correctAnswer: Energy oscillates (tanks) between L and C at resonance
questions.explanation: At resonance, current circulates between L and C internally. Energy 'tanks' — oscillates back and forth — without external dissipation (ideally).

questions.prompt: The normalised transfer function 1/[1+jQ(ω/ω₀−ω₀/ω)] applies to:
options: Both series and parallel (universal second-order form)
questions.correctAnswer: Both series and parallel (universal second-order form)
questions.explanation: This bandpass form is universal for any second-order resonant system — series, parallel, mechanical, or acoustic.
